summary: Crown defects include fractured cusps, cracked crowns, failed restorations, and significant structural compromise. Treatment depends on extent: minor defects may be repaired with composite bonding, while extensive damage requires full-coverage crown restoration. Porcelain-fused-to-metal and all-ceramic crowns have 10-year survival rates of 90-95%. If the defect extends subgingivally, crown lengthening surgery may be needed before restoration.
risk_factors: Large existing restorations weakening remaining tooth structure
History of root canal treatment (reduced tooth flexibility)
alternatives: Direct composite restoration for minor cuspal defects
Porcelain onlay for moderate structural loss (more conservative than full crown)
Post-and-core buildup with crown for endodontically treated teeth
Extraction and implant if tooth is non-restorable
referral_summary: General dentists perform most crown restorations. Referral to a prosthodontist is recommended for complex cases involving occlusal rehabilitation, aesthetic zone crowns, or when crown lengthening is needed. Endodontic evaluation may be required if pulp involvement is suspected.
patient_education: A crown defect means part of your tooth's structure is damaged or weakened. Your dentist may recommend a crown, which is a custom-made cap that fits over your tooth to restore its shape, strength, and appearance. The procedure typically requires two visits — one to prepare the tooth and take impressions, and another to place the permanent crown. Modern crowns are tooth-colored and very durable.
